turns.text: Hello Delena, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're thinking about starting a family, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*shifts a little in my seat, tucking a strand of hair behind my ear*

Yeah, okay. I appreciate you asking. It's... it's kind of nice to talk about this, actually. I don't really tell people at work or anything because it feels like they'd just laugh, you know? Like I'm too young or something.

*takes a breath*

So right now I'm not pregnant yet — I'm in that waiting stage. And I know that probably sounds weird to some people, like why am I even thinking about maternity care when I'm not even expecting? But I've been doing a lot of research, trying to understand my body better, because... I don't know. It feels like if I'm going to do this, I should do it right.

I've been tracking everything — my temperature, my cycle, all of it. I brought charts to the clinic thinking maybe the doctor would see how serious I am, how much I want this. But I got this polite smile, like I'm just another young girl with "plenty of time." And maybe they're right, technically. But I don't feel like I have time. The rent keeps going up and I'm scrambling for full-time work that actually pays, and I just... I keep thinking about my future, about whether I can even afford to have a family.

*looks down*

So yeah, that's kind of where I'm at. Does that make sense?

Before you started trying to conceive, what did you expect the process of getting support or guidance from healthcare professionals to look like? And thinking back now, what did that support actually look like in reality?

*leans back, rubbing my arms*

Okay, so... I thought there would be more of a conversation, honestly. Like, I imagined going to a doctor and being able to say, "Hey, I'm thinking about starting a family in the next year or so. What should I know? What should I be doing now?" And I thought they'd actually want to know about my health, my lifestyle, my concerns. Not just... checkboxes.

*voice gets a little quieter*

What actually happened was pretty different. I went in for a regular checkup and mentioned I was thinking about pregnancy, and the nurse was nice enough, but she just kind of rattled off the standard stuff — take prenatal vitamins, don't smoke, blah blah blah. When I started asking more specific questions about my cycle and timing, she seemed a little... dismissive? Like I was overthinking it.

*picks at my cuticles*

I brought my temperature charts and everything. I've been tracking for months. I thought that would show I'm taking this seriously, that I'm not just some reckless kid. But she just glanced at them and said, "You're young and healthy, so you probably don't need to worry." And I get it — statistically I'm fine. But that's not really... comforting? Because my situation isn't just about biology, right? It's about money, about stability, about whether I can actually make this happen.

*looks up*

I wanted to feel like they *got* that. That they understood I'm not just worried about getting pregnant — I'm worried about everything else too.
Before trying to conceive, what did you expect your own emotional readiness to feel like? And how did your emotional state actually compare to those expectations once the process began?

*pauses, thinking*

That's... a harder question than I expected.

Honestly, I think I imagined it would feel more... triumphant? Like, I'd wake up one day and just *know* I was ready. I'd feel excited and prepared and confident. In my head, emotional readiness looked like having a plan, having the money saved, having a partner lined up, all of that. A checklist that added up to "yes, now is the time."

*voice gets smaller*

The reality is... messier. I don't have most of those things, and I still feel this pull toward it. This ache that doesn't really care about my timeline or my bank account. Some days I wake up and I'm terrified — like, what am I doing? I can barely afford my apartment. How am I going to afford a baby? And then other days I'm just... sad. Like my body's ticking and time's moving and I'm stuck in this in-between place where nothing feels ready but waiting feels impossible.

*rubs my face*

I thought I'd feel more in control, I guess. But instead I'm hyperaware of everything — every month that passes, every temperature spike that doesn't mean anything, every pregnancy announcement on social media. It's exhausting. And nobody really prepared me for that emotional whiplash, you know? The doctors just see the biology. They don't see the anxiety of someone my age who's already feeling like I'm running out of time.

*looks at you directly*

I'm not sure I'm emotionally ready. But I'm also not sure that feeling matters anymore.
What was missing from the support available to you emotionally at this stage — from professionals, your partner, or your wider network?

*exhales slowly, looking away*

Everything, kind of. But let me break it down.

From the doctors — I needed someone to actually *listen*. Not dismiss me because I'm twenty-two. I needed them to acknowledge that my concerns are real, even if they're not medical emergencies. Someone to say, "Hey, I hear you. This is stressful and it's valid to feel anxious about it." Instead I got treated like I was overreacting.

*fidgets with my hands*

And the partner thing... *laughs bitterly* I don't have one. That's part of the whole problem. I'm doing this alone, which makes everything feel harder and scarier. I don't have someone to talk to at night when I'm spiraling about whether my body's working right, or whether I'm being stupid for wanting this without stable employment, without... security.

*voice gets quieter*

As for my wider network — I can't really talk about this with people. My mom would probably freak out. My friends are out clubbing and figuring out their careers, and I'm here obsessing over cervical mucus. There's shame in that, you know? Like I'm supposed to be carefree at twenty-two, not planning for pregnancy like some desperate woman in her late thirties.

*looks up*

What I really needed was someone to help me untangle all of this — the medical stuff *and* the emotional stuff *and* the practical stuff. Someone who sees me as a whole person, not just a body that needs prenatal vitamins. Someone who could help me figure out if this is actually the right time, or if I'm just scared of losing the opportunity.
Before conception, what did you expect to find when you looked for information online or through apps about fertility and getting pregnant? And what did you actually find — was it what you needed?

*leans forward, getting animated*

Oh man, okay. So I thought I'd find... clarity, I guess? Like, reliable information that would help me understand my body better. Scientific stuff. Real data about timing and fertility windows and what actually matters.

*pulls out phone reflexively, then stops*

And I did find a lot of that — there are amazing resources out there. I've read studies, tracked everything in apps, learned more about my cycle than most people know in a lifetime. The information is there if you want it badly enough.

*expression darkens*

But here's what I didn't expect: how *scary* it all is. Every article about fertility talks about declining eggs after thirty, about how your window closes, about how you should've started thinking about this earlier. And I'm twenty-two reading this stuff like it's a countdown timer. The medical information is accurate but it's... it's relentless, you know?

*voice gets frustrated*

And then there's all the other stuff mixed in — the Instagram wellness culture, the "fertility hacks," people selling supplements I can't afford anyway. It's hard to separate what's real science from what's just someone trying to make money off desperate people like me.

*sits back*

What I actually needed was context. Someone to say, "Yes, this information is accurate, but here's what it actually means for *your* life. Here's what's worth worrying about and what isn't." Instead I got buried in data and worst-case scenarios.

*quietly*

I'm more informed but way more anxious than before.
What was the biggest gap between what digital sources told you and what your healthcare provider told you?

*laughs, but there's frustration in it*

Oh, there's a huge gap. Like, night and day.

The apps and the studies I found — they treat me like I'm an intelligent person trying to understand something complex. They break down hormone cycles, they explain what LH surges actually mean, they give you the science behind why timing matters. I spent hours learning about my own reproductive system. I felt *empowered* by that information, like I was taking control of something I could actually control.

*shifts uncomfortably*

And then I go to the clinic and it's like... none of that matters? The doctor basically told me not to overthink it. "Just have sex regularly and you'll get pregnant." Like I'm being neurotic for wanting to understand the *why* and the *when*. There's this huge disconnect between what the evidence says and what my healthcare provider acts like I should care about.

*voice gets more agitated*

The internet told me I should be proactive, informed, tracking my data. My doctor made me feel like I was wasting time and energy on something that shouldn't need that much attention at my age. One made me feel smart. The other made me feel stupid for asking questions.

*pauses*

And the worst part? I don't know who to trust. Is the doctor right and I'm just anxious? Or am I right to be taking this seriously and my doctor just doesn't have time for me? 

*quietly frustrated*

That gap between the two worlds — it's left me confused and more anxious than either one alone would've.
If you had a pre-existing health condition before pregnancy, what did you expect the healthcare system to do to help you prepare? And what actually happened when you sought that help?

*pauses, looking a bit uncertain*

Oh, um... I don't have any pre-existing conditions, actually. I'm pretty healthy — that's the thing. Low risk, normal BMI, no major medical history. The doctors made sure to tell me that multiple times, like it's supposed to reassure me.

*shifts in seat*

And honestly? Part of me wonders if that's why I'm not getting taken seriously. Because on paper, I'm the "easy" patient. No complications, nothing to manage, just a young, healthy girl who should be able to get pregnant without any fuss. So why does she need all this support and attention?

*voice gets quieter, more vulnerable*

But being "healthy" doesn't mean I don't have needs. It doesn't mean I'm not anxious or struggling with the practical side of all this. I think maybe the healthcare system assumes that if you're low-risk medically, you don't need much else. You just... do it.

*looks down*

I guess if I *did* have something — like PCOS or endometriosis or whatever — maybe they'd actually pay attention to me? Maybe they'd want to have a real conversation instead of just handing me a pamphlet about folic acid. It's weird to wish I had a medical problem just so someone would take my concerns seriously.

*meets your eyes*

But I don't. So here I am, the "easy" patient that nobody's worried about, feeling completely lost anyway.
Before you conceived, what did you expect your partner's involvement in the preconception process to look like? And how did their actual involvement compare to what you'd imagined?

*goes very still, then looks away*

Oh. Um.

*long pause*

I don't have a partner. That's... that's actually a big part of this whole thing, and I'm not sure how to talk about it without sounding pathetic.

*fidgets with hands*

I think what I expected was different, honestly. In my head, I imagined doing this *with* someone, you know? Like, both of us going to appointments, both of us reading about fertility, both of us being excited and nervous together. Someone to hold my hand through the anxiety, someone who's as invested in this as I am.

*voice gets quieter, more raw*

The reality is I'm doing this alone. And that's... that's a lot harder than I thought it would be. Every month I'm tracking my cycle by myself, wondering if this is the month, worrying about whether I can even afford to raise a kid without support. I'm the only one thinking about this. Nobody's reassuring me at night when I'm spiraling.

*looks up, a bit defensive*

I know that probably sounds weird — like, why am I even thinking about pregnancy without a partner? And believe me, I've asked myself that a thousand times. But the thing is... I want this. And I'm terrified that waiting for the "right" partner and the "right" time means I'll never get to be a mom.

*voice cracks slightly*

So yeah. His involvement looks like... nothing. Because he doesn't exist yet. And that's the scariest part of all this.
If you experienced a previous pregnancy loss, what did you expect the healthcare system to offer you when you decided to try again? And what support was actually available to you?

*shakes head slowly*

I haven't... I mean, I've never been pregnant. So there's no loss to grieve, if that's what you're asking.

*pauses, then adds quietly*

But I think about it sometimes. Like, what if I finally do get pregnant and something goes wrong? What happens then? Will they actually support me through that, or will I just get shuffled through the system like I don't matter?

*looks down*

I don't know what that would feel like — losing something I've wanted this badly. I'm already grieving months that have passed without it happening, even though logically I know I'm young and I have time. But that doesn't make the disappointment feel any less real each month.

*voice gets softer*

I guess what scares me is that if I did experience a loss, I'd be alone with it. Just like I'm alone with all of this now. There's no partner to process it with, no family I've told, no community of people going through the same thing. And based on how dismissive healthcare providers have been so far, I'm not confident they'd take it seriously either.

*meets your eyes*

They'd probably just tell me, "You're young, you can try again." Like that fixes anything. Like the emotional part doesn't matter as long as biologically I'm fine.

*quietly*

That terrifies me more than the idea of a loss itself, honestly.
Was there a point where you felt your previous loss was not being taken seriously enough in the context of your new pregnancy attempt?

*looks confused, then clarifies gently*

I think there might be some confusion — I haven't had a previous loss. I've never been pregnant at all. This is all preconception stuff for me right now.

*shifts slightly*

I mean, I get what you're asking about, and it's a real thing that happens to people. But that's not my story. My story is... I'm stuck in this waiting phase. I'm grieving months that pass without pregnancy happening, but that's different from actual miscarriage or loss.

*pauses thoughtfully*

Though honestly? Part of why I brought it up is because I'm terrified of what *would* happen if I did lose a pregnancy. Based on how I've been treated so far — dismissed, not taken seriously, treated like I'm overreacting — I don't have a lot of confidence in the system to hold space for that kind of grief.

*voice gets quieter*

So maybe the question for me isn't about a previous loss. It's more like... I'm already experiencing this sense of loss and disappointment every month, and nobody's really validating that. So if something bigger happened, I'm not sure they'd do any better.

*looks at you*

Does that make sense? I'm worried about support I might need in the future based on how I'm being treated right now.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*takes a deep breath, thinking*

Yeah, actually. There's something I keep coming back to.

*leans forward*

I think what's hardest is feeling like I have to choose between two impossible things. I can be responsible — track my body, educate myself, prepare — but then I'm anxious and obsessive. Or I can let it go and just live my life, but then I feel like I'm wasting time and being reckless about my future.

*voice gets more emotional*

And nobody in the healthcare system is helping me figure out that balance. They're not asking, "Hey, how are you actually doing with all of this?" They're just... moving through their day. I'm not a crisis, so I don't get real support.

*pauses*

The money thing too. I need someone to acknowledge that this isn't just about biology for me. It's about whether I can afford to be a mother at all. And that's terrifying. I want to be told that it's okay to want this, that I don't have to have it all figured out and perfectly stable before I deserve that dream.

*voice gets quieter*

I guess what I want is for healthcare providers to see people like me — young, low-risk, low-income, solo — and actually *see* us. Not dismiss us. Not treat us like we have nothing to worry about when we're clearly worried about everything.

*looks directly at you*

Just... see me. Please.
